notes: An enclitic used to form a question. For enclitic see Ls 2, Gram. Note 3.
Frequently combined with "chin" to mean "very much/very many." This "chē" is the same character and meaning as the "chē" in "lōa-chē." Distinguish from "chē" of "chhiá* chē..."
example_english: I have a lot of money.

notes: If the subject is 2d person, this term is usually a greeting. "Bōe" here is enclitic.
example_english: Yes, I have eaten. (Subject omitted. This is an answer to "Chiah-pá bōe?")

example_poj: I chin hó. | I chin ài khòa*-chheh.
example_english: He is very good. | He really likes to read.
I don't like coffee. / I don't want coffee (now). (Lit., I don't like/want to drink coffee.)
to talk. | He loves to talk.

example_poj: Lim kún-chúi. | Lí ài lim kún-chúi bô·?
example_english: to drink boiled water. | Do you want a drink of water?

notes: "Kùi" will be used in a later lesson to mean "expensive". Using the honorific term "kùi" is a polite way of asking the name of an adult. It is not ordinarily spoken to younger people, never to children.
The "lâng" frequently changes tone, an exception to the rule that one-syllable nouns do not change tone.

english: no one / not one (person) / there is no one who
example_english: None of them wants to study.

english: there is someone who... / there are people who...
example_poj: Ū lâng ài lim kún-chúi bô·?
example_english: Does anyone want a drink of water?
What do you want to drink?

example_poj: Nĝ Siàn-si* bô sím-mih ài kóng-ōe. | Lâu Siàn-si* chin ài kóng-ōe.
example_english: Mr. N̂g is rather quiet (does not like to talk much / doesn't say much). | Mr. Lâu loves to talk.

english: clan name / family name / surname; to be surnamed
example_poj: Góa sè* Lîm. Lí kùi sè*?
example_english: My surname is Lîm. What's yours?

notes: The word for "wife" will be in Ls 4.

english: Sorry; Please excuse me; to be impolite
notes: = I beg your pardon for my want of politeness; a breaching of etiquette.
example_english: I'm very sorry (e.g., for making a mess of things).

notes: A religious sister; this term is not used to denote blood relationship.
A religious brother; this term is not used to denote blood relationship.

example_english: Please have some tea (OR: drinking water).

example_poj: Góa ài lim kún-chúi, bô ài lim tê-bí-tê.
example_english: I want to drink water, not tea.

notes: E.g., to read Scripture lesson in church; in this case we do not say "khòa*". tha̍k-chheh = to study. Used only of a student in reference to school books; by extension it means "to be a student". Distinguish from "khòa*-chheh" which means "to read a book" but not in the sense of studying.
example_poj: I chin ài kóng-ōe, bô ài tha̍k-chheh.
example_english: He likes to talk; he doesn't like studying.
